Name: task_interference_cancellation_precision_routing
Title: Task-Specific Geometric Interference Cancellation for Robust Precision Routing
Short Hypothesis: Task-specific geometric interference patterns destabilize precision routing decisions, and explicitly canceling these patterns improves robustness and efficiency under dynamic task distributions.
Related Work: Existing work on precision routing (e.g., Spectral Quantization Routing) focuses on activation geometry but does not explicitly address task-specific interference. Training-free geometric interference cancellation has been explored for in-context learning but not in the context of precision routing. This proposal bridges these gaps by modeling and canceling task-specific interference to enhance precision routing robustness.
Abstract: Precision routing promises significant computational savings by dynamically allocating numerical precision based on activation geometry. However, task-specific geometric interference—overlapping subspaces in multi-task settings—can destabilize routing decisions, leading to suboptimal precision allocation. We propose a training-free framework that explicitly models and cancels task-specific interference patterns, ensuring robust precision routing under dynamic task distributions. By computing local activation covariances and projecting onto task-specific subspaces, we isolate interference-free directions that guide precision allocation. We theoretically ground our approach in PAC-Bayes bounds, linking subspace stability to generalization under precision noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across multi-task benchmarks, our method achieves superior robustness and efficiency compared to existing precision routing baselines. This work bridges geometric interference cancellation with precision routing, offering a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference in dynamic task environments.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Multi-task benchmark evaluation. Test the framework on diverse multi-task datasets (e.g., SuperGLUE, WILDS) to measure robustness and efficiency under task interference.
Experiment 2: Dynamic task adaptation. Simulate task shifts during inference and evaluate precision allocation stability and accuracy retention.
Experiment 3: Ablation study. Remove interference cancellation to isolate its impact on precision routing performance.
Experiment 4: Latency analysis. Measure wall-clock latency and computational overhead to ensure scalability.
Metrics: Task-specific accuracy, precision allocation stability, latency, and computational overhead.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: Overhead from interference cancellation may outweigh precision savings.
Risk 2: Task-specific subspaces may not generalize well to unseen tasks.
Limitation: Requires access to task labels or embeddings, which may not always be available.
Limitation: Evaluated on single-GPU setups; scalability to larger systems remains untested.
